It’s tangible. Social media, though, feels like trying to judge the quality of a piece of lumber you can’t actually touch, and that’s what all this fake content is. It’s just sawdust and smoke. Because I get a bit anxious sometimes, especially when things are messy or unpredictable, the sheer volume of nonsense online is genuinely stressful. You see something big, something that sounds too good or too terrible to be true, and your brain just starts spinning, trying to figure out if it’s solid or just some flimsy veneer. My approach, and I think this is where most people trip up, isn't about trying to fact-check every single thing—that’s a full-time job, and I’ve got enough building to do. Instead, it’s about building a strong filter, like checking your tools before you start a cut. First, I focus on the source. Who is saying this? Is it a verified expert, or is it some random account that popped up last Tuesday? If the source feels flimsy, I treat the information like cheap plywood—you don't build anything important on it. Second, I look for confirmation, but I don't just accept it from the first place I see it. I might take a quick look at a couple of established, reputable news outlets, the kind that have a real track record, not just the loudest voices. If the story is huge, multiple solid sources will be talking about it; if it’s only coming from one place, especially one that’s overly emotional, my internal alarm bells go off. Honestly, sometimes the best management tool is just stepping away. If scrolling starts making my chest tight, if I start getting that low-level hum of worry about what might be true or false, I put the phone down. I go out, I take a walk, or maybe I mess around with the camera, focusing on something real, like the way the light hits the river. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, when you’re out on a job site, you deal with things that are real—the grain in the wood, the way the scaffolding holds up under stress. It’s tangible. Social media, though, feels like trying to judge the quality of a piece of lumber you can’t actually touch, and that’s what all this fake content is. It’s just sawdust and smoke. Because I get a bit anxious sometimes, especially when things are messy or unpredictable, the sheer volume of nonsense online is genuinely stressful. You see something big, something that sounds too good or too terrible to be true, and your brain just starts spinning, trying to figure out if it’s solid or just some flimsy veneer. My approach, and I think this is where most people trip up, isn't about trying to fact-check every single thing—that’s a full-time job, and I’ve got enough building to do. Instead, it’s about building a strong filter, like checking your tools before you start a cut. First, I focus on the source. Who is saying this? Is it a verified expert, or is it some random account that popped up last Tuesday? If the source feels flimsy, I treat the information like cheap plywood—you don't build anything important on it. Second, I look for confirmation, but I don't just accept it from the first place I see it. I might take a quick look at a couple of established, reputable news outlets, the kind that have a real track record, not just the loudest voices. If the story is huge, multiple solid sources will be talking about it; if it’s only coming from one place, especially one that’s overly emotional, my internal alarm bells go off. Honestly, sometimes the best management tool is just stepping away. If scrolling starts making my chest tight, if I start getting that low-level hum of worry about what might be true or false, I put the phone down. I go out, I take a walk, or maybe I mess around with the camera, focusing on something real, like the way the light hits the river. That’s how you keep the noise from messing up your head.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a conscientious, high-extraversion, slightly anxious 28-year-old carpenter. The tone should be practical, grounded, slightly skeptical, and reflective of someone who values tangible reality (carpentry) over digital ambiguity. The advice needs to be actionable but not overly academic. The focus should shift from 'solving' the problem (which is impossible) to 'managing' the anxiety it causes. The analogy of building/tools is key to align with the carpentry background.
